steps.title: The Knight Fork
steps.point: A fork attacks two pieces at once. Here Nc7+ is a 'royal fork': it checks the king on e8 and simultaneously attacks the rook on a8. The king must move, and you win the rook next move.

steps.title: The Pin
steps.point: A pin freezes a piece because moving it would expose something more valuable. Black's bishop on b4 pins White's knight on d2 to the king on e1 — the knight literally has no legal moves. Pinned pieces are weak; pile more attackers on them.

steps.title: The Skewer
steps.point: A skewer is a reverse pin: the more valuable piece is in front. Bc3+ checks the king on g7 along the long diagonal; when the king steps aside, the rook on h8 is captured. Line up enemy pieces on the same line to skewer them.

steps.title: The Fork in the opening
steps.point: Tactics hide in normal positions too. Watch the f7 square: it is defended only by the black king. Combinations built around a knight or bishop hitting f7 (the weakest square in the opening) win material or the king's safety — always calculate them.
